title: DAG-Aware Intelligence
tagline: Severity-rated blast radius, lineage traversal, critical-path timings
summary: dbt Wizard reasons over a live, queryable DAG graph (models, columns, tests, edges) backed by a local DuckDB index, not by reading files. Impact reports trace column- and node-level blast radius through the DAG with critical/high/medium/low severity ratings; lineage traversal walks upstream/downstream with hop-depth control, edge-type filtering, and column-level tracing; and bottleneck analysis surfaces critical-path nodes from per-node durations captured from the last run. The headline workflow: ask what breaks if a column is dropped or renamed, and the wizard returns a severity-rated list of downstream consumers plus the exact lineage path connecting them.
whyDifferent: A general AI coding agent answers "what references brand_name" with grep: a flat list of files containing that string, no ordering, no understanding of whether a match is a passthrough alias or a load-bearing join key. dbt Wizard answers "what breaks if I drop brand_name" by traversing the actual compiled DAG and warehouse catalog, returning each affected node with a severity rating and the lineage path that explains why. Grep cannot tell you that a fct_orders test will fail or that a downstream MetricFlow metric loses its dimension; the DAG index can. Severity comes from edge type, test coverage, and grain, not from text frequency.

capabilities.title: Column-level blast radius with severity
capabilities.detail: mcp__dbt_index__impact scoped to a single column (e.g. stg_products.brand_name) returns every downstream consumer rated critical/high/medium/low. Severity reflects how the column is used: a join key or test input ranks higher than a cosmetic passthrough alias.

capabilities.title: Severity from structure, not text frequency
capabilities.detail: Ratings derive from edge type, test coverage, grain dependency, and materialization in the DAG and warehouse catalog. A column referenced once as a primary join key outranks a column mentioned ten times in comments or unused select lists.

capabilities.title: Depth-controlled lineage traversal
capabilities.detail: mcp__dbt_index__lineage walks upstream or downstream with a max_depth hop cap, so an SE can ask for one hop (direct dependents) or full transitive closure. Direction is upstream, downstream, or both.

capabilities.title: Edge-type filtering and column-level lineage
capabilities.detail: Lineage can follow only ref edges, or include test and metric edges via edge_types, and can trace a single column field-to-field through the graph. This isolates the propagation path that actually matters for a given question.

capabilities.title: Critical-path bottleneck analysis
capabilities.detail: mcp__dbt_index__timings identifies the slowest nodes and the critical-path nodes gating total build time. The numbers are per-node durations and critical-path data from the last run, captured from dbt run results and events into the index, rather than guesses.

capabilities.title: Impact plus lineage in one answer
capabilities.detail: The headline workflow pairs impact (what breaks, how badly) with lineage (the exact path connecting target to consumer), so the SE sees both the severity-ranked consumer list and the structural reason behind each rating.

examplePrompts.prompt: What breaks if I drop brand_name from stg_products?
examplePrompts.intent: Column-level blast radius for a destructive change, with severity ranking and downstream consumers.

examplePrompts.prompt: Show me everything downstream of fct_orders, but only two hops out.
If I rename order_id in int_orders_enriched, what's the blast radius and which tests fail?
examplePrompts.intent: Rename impact with test-failure surfacing across the DAG.

examplePrompts.prompt: Trace the lineage of brand_name from source through to the gold layer.
What are the slowest nodes on the critical path of last night's build?
examplePrompts.intent: Critical-path bottleneck analysis from run timings.

examplePrompts.prompt: Only follow ref edges upstream from dim_customers, skip tests and metrics.
Give me the critical/high impact of a type change on amount in fct_orders, then show the path to each affected metric.
examplePrompts.intent: Type-change impact filtered to high severity, followed by lineage to metric consumers.

tools.name: mcp__dbt_index__impact
tools.purpose: Compute DAG-aware blast radius for a node or specific column, classifying every downstream consumer with a critical/high/medium/low severity rating derived from edge type, test coverage, grain dependency, and materialization.

params.name: node
params.desc: Target model/source whose change is being assessed, e.g. stg_products.

params.name: column
params.desc: Optional column to scope blast radius to a single field, e.g. brand_name. Omit for whole-node impact.

params.name: change_type
params.desc: Optional: drop, rename, type_change, or logic_change. Tunes severity heuristics.

params.name: max_depth
params.desc: Optional hop limit on downstream traversal. Defaults to full transitive closure.

params.name: scope
params.desc: Optional subgraph restriction, e.g. a layer (silver, gold) or tag selector.

tools.returns: Ordered list of affected nodes with severity (critical/high/medium/low), the dependency reason (join key, filter, passthrough, test input, metric dimension), affected tests, and a per-node hop distance. Includes a rollup count by severity tier.

tools.name: mcp__dbt_index__lineage
tools.purpose: Traverse the DAG upstream and/or downstream from a node or column, returning the ordered path of nodes and edges so an SE can see exactly how a change propagates.

params.name: node
params.desc: Starting node for traversal, e.g. fct_orders.

params.name: direction
params.desc: upstream, downstream, or both. Defaults to both.

params.name: max_depth
params.desc: Hop-depth control; caps how many edges out the traversal walks.

params.name: edge_types
params.desc: Optional edge-type filter: ref, source, test, metric. Restricts which dependency kinds are followed.

params.name: column
params.desc: Optional column-level lineage, e.g. trace brand_name field-to-field through the graph.

tools.returns: Ordered node/edge path with hop distances and edge types. Column-level mode returns the field-to-field transformation chain.

tools.name: mcp__dbt_index__timings
tools.purpose: Report build performance from the last run: slowest nodes or critical-path bottlenecks, so SEs can find what is actually gating the pipeline.

params.name: view
params.desc: Optional: slowest or critical_path. slowest ranks nodes by duration; critical_path returns the nodes on the longest dependency path gating total build time.

params.name: node
params.desc: Optional node to scope the timing report to a single model and its path.

params.name: limit
params.desc: Optional cap on number of nodes returned.

tools.returns: Ranked nodes with per-node execution durations and a flag for critical-path membership. Durations and critical-path data are captured from the last run's dbt run results and events into the index.

interaction.scenario: An SE on a customer call is asked the classic refactor question: a staging model has a column the team wants to remove, and they need to know what will break before touching it. The SE types the question into dbt Wizard live.
interaction.userPrompt: We want to drop brand_name from stg_products. What breaks, and how bad is it?

events.body: We want to drop brand_name from stg_products. What breaks, and how bad is it?
This is a destructive column change. I'll run a column-scoped impact report against the DAG to get severity-rated downstream consumers, rather than just listing files that mention the string.

events.code: {"target": "stg_products.brand_name", "rollup": {"critical": 1, "high": 1, "medium": 1, "low": 2}, "affected": [{"node": "int_orders_enriched", "severity": "critical", "reason": "join key + not_null test input", "hop": 1, "tests": ["not_null_int_orders_enriched_brand_name"]}, {"node": "fct_orders", "severity": "high", "reason": "grain-carrying passthrough used by metric", "hop": 2, "tests": []}, {"node": "metric:orders_by_brand", "severity": "medium", "reason": "MetricFlow dimension", "hop": 3, "tests": []}, {"node": "dim_products", "severity": "low", "reason": "cosmetic alias passthrough", "hop": 1, "tests": []}, {"node": "rpt_brand_summary", "severity": "low", "reason": "unused select-list column", "hop": 4, "tests": []}]}

events.body: One critical hit: int_orders_enriched uses brand_name as a join key and has a not_null test on it, so dropping the column breaks the build and the test. Let me pull the exact lineage path to that critical consumer and the downstream metric so I can show how it propagates.

Confirmed: the propagation chain is stg_products -> int_orders_enriched (join + test) -> fct_orders -> orders_by_brand metric. The low-severity nodes (dim_products alias, rpt_brand_summary unused column) would compile fine after a trivial edit. I'll synthesize.

Dropping stg_products.brand_name has a 1 critical / 1 high / 1 medium / 2 low blast radius.

CRITICAL - int_orders_enriched (1 hop): brand_name is a join key here and has a not_null test (not_null_int_orders_enriched_brand_name). Dropping it breaks the build and fails the test. This is the one you must fix first.

HIGH - fct_orders (2 hops): carries brand_name as a grain passthrough that feeds a metric. Compiles only after int_orders_enriched is fixed.

MEDIUM - orders_by_brand (3 hops): a MetricFlow dimension that disappears, breaking any dashboard sliced by brand.

LOW - dim_products and rpt_brand_summary: a cosmetic alias and an unused select-list column; trivial to clean up.

Propagation path: stg_products -> int_orders_enriched (ref + test) -> fct_orders (ref) -> orders_by_brand (metric). Recommended order: repoint or rename the int_orders_enriched join and drop its test, then fct_orders, then the metric definition. Note grep would have shown all five as equal 'references' and missed the test failure and the lost metric dimension entirely.

seNotes: Lead with the grep contrast. The demo lands when the audience sees that impact returns severity plus a reason (join key, test input, metric dimension) while grep returns an undifferentiated file list. Say it out loud: 'grep counts mentions; impact understands consequences.'

Severity comes from DAG structure and the warehouse catalog, not text frequency. A column referenced once as a join key outranks one mentioned ten times in comments. Be ready to explain the inputs: edge type, test coverage, grain, materialization.
Pair impact with lineage in the same answer. Impact says what breaks and how badly; lineage shows the exact path. Showing both is what makes the answer trustworthy to a skeptical data engineer.

Show edge-type filtering and max_depth live. Toggling edge_types to include test and metric edges, or capping max_depth to one hop for 'just direct dependents,' demonstrates this is graph traversal, not a flat search.

timings reports per-node durations and critical-path data captured from the last run's dbt run results and events into the index, so they are grounded in real execution rather than estimates. If asked about cost or performance, pivot to the critical_path view to show the nodes actually gating total build time rather than just the slowest in isolation.
